name: Reflexive Verbs
summary: Daily-routine verbs that take a reflexive pronoun, like se laver (to wash oneself).
explanation:
Reflexive verbs describe an action done to oneself and carry a reflexive pronoun that matches the subject. Se laver (to wash) becomes je me lave (I wash myself), tu te laves, il se lave.

The pronouns are: me, te, se, nous, vous, se. They sit directly before the verb: nous nous levons (we get up). Before a vowel, me, te, se shorten to m', t', s': je m'appelle (my name is, literally 'I call myself').

Many everyday routine verbs are reflexive: se lever (to get up), se coucher (to go to bed), se reposer (to rest), s'habiller (to get dressed).

examples.native: Je me lève à sept heures.
examples.en: I get up at seven o'clock.

examples.native: Ils se promènent dans le parc.

commonMistakes: Dropping the reflexive pronoun: it is je me lave, not je lave (which means 'I wash something else').
Not matching the pronoun to the subject: it is nous nous levons, not nous se levons.
Forgetting to elide before a vowel: it is je m'appelle, not je me appelle.
Placing the pronoun after the verb. It comes directly before the conjugated verb.
Treating every routine verb as reflexive. Only certain verbs carry the pronoun.
tip: If the action loops back onto the subject, you need a reflexive pronoun. Match me, te, se to the subject every time.
